Colibrí
Music born on the waterways of Venice
Enter

The Voyage

Colibrí was born on the water. Before there was music, there was Venice — the slow pull of the oar, the way sound carries across a canal at dusk, the silence beneath a bridge before it gives way to light on the other side. Years spent as a gondolier shaped something that couldn’t stay quiet. The rhythm of the water became rhythm. The echo became melody. The journey became the song.

Every passage through those ancient archways was a crossing from one world into another — shadow into gold, stone into sky. That feeling lives in the music now. Each track is a voyage, an invitation to step into the boat and let the current carry you somewhere you haven’t been.

The name itself is a crossing: colibrí — the hummingbird — a creature that moves between worlds, that hovers in the space between stillness and flight. Like the music, it never quite lands. It just keeps moving, iridescent, toward the light.

Frequently Asked Questions

  • Colibrí means “hummingbird” in various languages, including Italian and Spanish.

  • A gondolier is someone who rows a traditional Venetian boat called a gondola.

  • Yes, there are several authentic Venetian vessels here.

  • Yes. Please feel free to contact me directly if you would like to book a ride with me.

  • I usually play along with backing tracks that I record myself. It sounds like a full band, but it’s just me. I do bring extra instruments just in case the audience wants to join in, however!

Not-So-FAQs

  • I was born here in North America, but I have heritage from around the world, including South America and Europe.

  • I speak six languages either fluently or conversationally and a smattering of others. In no particular order, my strongest languages are English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Italian, and Esperanto.

  • It is a language created by the linguist L. L. Zamenhof. It is easy to learn and fun to speak. I learned the basics of Esperanto on Duolingo.

  • Good observation. That’s because I grew up speaking Spanish as well as English.

  • No, I consciously refrain from using AI whatsoever in any part of the music-making process. This means I don’t use it to write lyrics, melodies, chord progressions, or anything else. I make music with my hands and my mind.

  • I record my music into an audio interface called a Scarlett by the company Focusrite. My preferred DAW (Digital Audio Workstation) is Reaper.

  • I am not currently available for private lessons, but I plan to post some instructional videos available for free on YouTube.
